title: Managing and conditioning a breeding buck
subtitle: Keeping a meat rabbit buck in good reproductive condition.
excerpt: A breeding buck in poor condition produces fewer successful matings. He needs a clean pen, regular but not continuous breeding use, a diet that maintains weight without producing fat, and a seasonal schedule that matches the doe's reproductive peak. An overused or over-conditioned buck is one of the most common sources of low kit numbers.
sourceNotes: British Rabbit Council breeding guidance, therabbitclub.net. FAO Small-scale rabbit production manual (2009). Morisse, J. P., rabbit breeding and management, French Rabbit Breeding Research Institute.

glossaryTerms.definition: An intact male rabbit used for stud purposes. Kept separately from does except during supervised mating.
The production of sperm in the testes. Suppressed in bucks by heat above 30°C, nutritional deficiency, illness, or stress. Recovery takes approximately six to seven weeks after the stressor resolves.

content.text: works best when kept separate from does between matings, in lean but healthy body condition, and used at a steady rate. Overuse and underuse are both problems. An overused buck loses fertility within weeks. A buck left unused for more than twelve weeks may produce poor sperm because the body slows

The buck's pen should be at the end of a run rather than between two doe pens, continual exposure to adjacent does disrupts his feeding and resting patterns. He should be able to smell and occasionally see the does but not be in constant stimulated proximity.

In summer, the pen must be shaded. A buck's testes are held outside the body and are sensitive to heat: sustained temperatures above 30°C suppress sperm production for six to seven weeks. A cool space in July and August is more important for fertility than any nutritional supplement.

Feed the buck a maintenance ration of good-quality pellets plus ad-lib hay. Unlike a doe in late pregnancy or lactation, he does not need a high-energy ration; an overfat buck has reduced libido and mounting ability. Aim for a lean, muscular condition, firm along the spine, ribs barely palpable under a thin covering.

A mature buck can serve up to five does per week without showing fertility decline, provided he is in good condition and resting between matings. For a small colony of four to six does, one mating per day at most, with two or three days off per week, is a sensible pattern.
Always bring the doe to the buck's cage for mating, not the other way around. A doe in her own pen will defend it and may injure the buck. The mating procedure is covered in the
Check the buck monthly using the

technique. Feel both testes for size and symmetry. A change in testicular size or hard lumps warrants veterinary investigation. Check the genitals for any discharge or sores, which can be signs of venereal spirochaetosis (rabbit syphilis), a bacterial infection that spreads at mating and requires antibiotic treatment.
